paragraphs.text:
# 🧲 The Lanthanide Contraction in Action: EDTA Stability Constants Across the Rare Earths

One of the most elegant demonstrations of the **lanthanide contraction** is how stability constants for a single ligand change systematically across the 4f series. Here's the complete set of log β₁ values for the 1:1 complex of each Ln³⁺ with **EDTA** (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid), all measured under consistent conditions:

**Conditions:** 25 °C, I = 0.1 M KNO₃, aqueous solution
**Equilibrium:** M³⁺ + L⁴⁻ ⇌ ML⁻ (log β₁)

| # | Metal Ion | Atomic Number | log β₁ | Δ from La³⁺ |
|---|-----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | La³⁺ | 57 | **15.50** | — |
| 2 | Ce³⁺ | 58 | **15.98** | +0.48 |
| 3 | Pr³⁺ | 59 | **16.40** | +0.90 |
| 4 | Nd³⁺ | 60 | **16.61** | +1.11 |
| 5 | Sm³⁺ | 62 | **17.14** | +1.64 |
| 6 | Eu³⁺ | 63 | **17.35** | +1.85 |
| 7 | Gd³⁺ | 64 | **17.37** | +1.87 |
| 8 | Tb³⁺ | 65 | **17.93** | +2.43 |
| 9 | Dy³⁺ | 66 | **18.30** | +2.80 |
| 10 | Ho³⁺ | 67 | **18.62** | +3.12 |
| 11 | Er³⁺ | 68 | **18.85** | +3.35 |
| 12 | Tm³⁺ | 69 | **19.32** | +3.82 |
| 13 | Yb³⁺ | 70 | **19.51** | +4.01 |
| 14 | Lu³⁺ | 71 | **19.83** | +4.33 |
| | **Y³⁺** | 39 | **18.09** | +2.59 |
| | **Sc³⁺** | 21 | **23.10** | +7.60 |

paragraphs.text:
## Key Observations

📈 **Monotonic increase La → Lu:** Log β₁ rises steadily from **15.50** (La³⁺) to **19.83** (Lu³⁺) — a span of **4.33 log units**, meaning Lu-EDTA is over **20,000× more stable** than La-EDTA. This directly reflects the lanthanide contraction: as 4f electrons are added across the series, they poorly shield nuclear charge, causing ionic radii to shrink. Smaller ions bind EDTA's six donor atoms (2N + 4O) more tightly.

🔍 **The "Gadolinium Break":** Notice the unusually small jump from Eu³⁺ (17.35) to Gd³⁺ (17.37) — only **+0.02 log units** — compared to the typical ~0.3–0.6 step between neighbors. This subtle plateau near the half-filled 4f⁷ shell (Gd³⁺) is the famous **gadolinium break**, reflecting the extra stabilization of the half-filled f-shell that slightly reduces the effective charge density gain.

🧪 **Y³⁺ fits between Tb³⁺ and Dy³⁺:** Yttrium's log β₁ of 18.09 slots neatly between Tb (17.93) and Dy (18.30), consistent with Y³⁺ having an ionic radius (~0.90 Å) nearly identical to Ho³⁺. This is why Y is always grouped with the heavy rare earths in separation chemistry.

⚡ **Sc³⁺ is the outlier:** With log β₁ = **23.10**, scandium's EDTA complex is over **3 orders of magnitude more stable** than even Lu-EDTA. Sc³⁺ is much smaller (0.745 Å) than any lanthanide, creating an exceptionally strong electrostatic interaction with EDTA's donor atoms.

paragraphs.text:
## Database Coverage at a Glance

The SRD-46 database contains extensive data for all rare earth M³⁺ ions:

| Metal Ion | Ligands Studied | Total VLM Records |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|
| La³⁺ | 297 | 931 |
| Gd³⁺ | 288 | 897 |
| Eu³⁺ | 267 | 870 |
| Sm³⁺ | 265 | 844 |
| Nd³⁺ | 263 | 794 |
| Er³⁺ | 241 | 792 |
| Yb³⁺ | 235 | 771 |
| Dy³⁺ | 252 | 758 |
| Pr³⁺ | 231 | 723 |
| Tb³⁺ | 235 | 709 |
| Ho³⁺ | 230 | 705 |
| Lu³⁺ | 234 | 671 |
| Tm³⁺ | 223 | 633 |
| Ce³⁺ | 188 | 567 |
| Y³⁺ | 182 | 533 |
| Sc³⁺ | 44 | 156 |

Notably, the light lanthanides (La, Gd, Eu) have the richest datasets, while Sc³⁺ has far fewer measurements — reflecting both its distinct chemistry and its relative scarcity in classical coordination studies.

> *The systematic trend in EDTA stability constants is precisely why EDTA-based complexometric titrations and ion-exchange chromatography can separate individual rare earths — the small but consistent differences in log β provide the thermodynamic basis for selectivity.*

*Source: NIST Standard Reference Database 46 (SRD-46)*
